observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management prepare and submit within the prescribed period the BRS together with the bank statements and attachments and to promptly prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items to arrive at a correct balance for the Cash in Bank account. Further, for Management to make proper adjustments in the books for the closure of LBP CA No. 3872100481.

We recommended that Management ensure that officers/employees who perform duties requiring control, custody or possession of government funds or property be   appropriately bonded in accordance with the law.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer orient the AO in maintaining, ruling and footing the prescribed cashbook and accompanying records and in accomplishing the required certification therein at the end of each month, pursuant to Section 28 of the Manual on NGAS for Local Government Units, Volume II.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to identify the specific sources of funds and establish whether these were essentially idle funds in accordance with Sections 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382. In cases when there are idle funds identified, investing such should have prior authority of the Sanggunian and the approval of the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that:
a) 	The Property and Accounting Units undertake the necessary reconciliation procedures as outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the correct balance of the PPE and record the necessary adjustments;
b) 	The Management reconstitute the Inventory Committee and furnish the Audit Team a copy of the Executive Order reconstituting the same; and
c) 	Henceforth, the members of the Inventory Committee certify the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Require the Accountable Officers and concerned offices to immediately submit the lacking documentary requirements to enable the evaluation of their requests for relief from accountability;
b. 	Direct the GSO to prepare the required reports to present lost and missing properties; and
c. 	Direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the lost and damaged properties in Cagsawa Ruins Park and Resort.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
a. 	Cause the preparation of the IIRUP by the Property Officer/Unit to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties; and
b. 	Direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require:
a. 	The GSO to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map and keep a complete LRNPCs for all roads and its components;
b. 	The Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and
c. 	The Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network and reconcile the same with the LRNPCs and the result of the physical inventory taking at the end of each year and submit upon request of the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that Management submit henceforth, copies of all perfected government contracts involving acquisition of land including all supporting documents within five working days from execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate timely auditorial and technical review thereof.
Further, for Management to provide a copy of the DENR Clearance and CAR to the Audit Team considering that considerable length of time has already passed from its payment. Also, to apprise the Audit Team of the status of transfer of title in the name of the LGU for this land acquisition.
We recommended that the Municipality expedite the securement of the title to the land so as to have an unimpeded exercise of all legal rights as the owner of the land.
We recommended that Management address this issue on the absence of right-of-way so as not to affect the feasibility of using the subject property for the development projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management identify the creditors comprising the unliquidated payables in CY 2021 to establish the validity of the accounts payable, otherwise, the same must be reverted if no valid claims exist.

We recommended that Management continue its implementation of the vaccination and booster dose program and widen the scope of its vaccine information drive to strengthen its constituents’ knowledge and protection against hospitalization and death from COVID-19.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Require the MHO to render a report to the Municipal Accountant on the receipt, issuance, and balances of vaccines directly received from outside sources; and
b. 	For the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MHO, to determine the full cost of ending inventory of vaccines as at year end and henceforth recognize and record future receipts and issuances of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
The Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to exert more efforts to collect the rentals and business taxes from delinquent market stall occupants using the procedures provided under the Contract of Lease and those included in the internal policy of the Municipality and enforce the civil remedies provided by the present Revenue Code, if necessary, in order to generate additional income that will be used for the implementation of programs, projects and activities of the Municipality; and
The Municipal Treasurer to periodically provide the Market Administrator of the list of delinquent stallholders and the amount still due and demandable for monitoring purposes, and strictly enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the market stall contracts relative to the automatic revocation or cancellation of the contract upon non-compliance of the terms and conditions stipulated in the Contract of Lease.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Require the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Licensing Office the list of all delinquent business owners before the ensuing year commences;
b. 	Require the Licensing Office to closely monitor that all business establishments have paid all permits necessary to commence and continue its operations within the reglementary period; thereafter prepare a report to the Office of the Mayor on business establishments who are operating illegally after their conduct of inspection; and
c. 	Enforce its police power by issuing a closure order for business establishments who are operating illegally.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. 	The Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the List of Delinquent Market Stallholders with amount due and penalties before the end of each year to facilitate timely recording of appropriate receivable and income accounts; and
b. 	The Municipal Accountant to recognize the total unpaid rentals, business taxes, surcharges and interests as of December 31, 2022 in the books of the Municipality to the appropriate receivable and income/equity account, in compliance with the provisions of PPSAS 13 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management formulate thru an appropriate ordinance from the Sanggunian Bayan the fixed rental fees and charges for the use of facilities, taking into consideration the expenses to be incurred in providing and maintaining the venue for lease.

We recommended that Management direct the issuance of a Contract of Lease in CY 2023 to qualified lessees, incorporating therein the duties and responsibilities of the lessees as well as other necessary stipulations to safeguard the interest of the Municipality.
Henceforth, the Municipality should see to it that contracts are properly prepared and issued in all its lease agreements.
We recommended that Management strengthen its Internal Control System relating to the collection of revenues by adopting the standards prescribed in Title 2, Book III of the GAAM.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer:
a. 	With the help of the Municipal Assessor, create strategies to intensify tax collection campaign in order to increase local resources which would help finance the delivery of basic services and facilities;
b. 	 Regularly update the list of delinquent real property tax thus create impact on the total amount due for collection; and
c. 	Coordinate or request the Provincial Office to send final demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and avail of the remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended the Management to take the necessary actions for the revision of the Schedule of Fair Market Values of real properties in the Municipality and publication of the same to avoid further loss of additional income from the increased assessment.

We recommended that the Municipal Assessor henceforth request the concerned Registrar of Deeds to furnish their Office every year a copy of the abstract of the Registry of Property, accompanied by copies of corresponding deeds of sale, donation, or partition, or other forms of alienation pursuant to the above-cited Manual.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Require the Municipal Human Resource Management Office to assess the existing organizational structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be established and submit a report with recommendation thereon to the Local Chief Executive (LCE) for approval by appropriate bodies;
b. 	Create policies and guidelines to address the necessity and propriety of hiring JOs taking into consideration the existing plantilla personnel; and
c. 	Require applicants to submit primary personal identification, undergo an interview and evaluate the performance of each personnel that would give assurance that the risks identified above are addressed.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Require the maintenance of attendance logbooks or any other means of recording attendance indicating respective names and signatures as well as the time of arrival and departure from the office assigned in support to the manual DTR; and
b. 	State in the Job Order, Contract of Service or Memorandum of Agreement the duties and responsibilities, expected outputs, and the rate per day of the workers.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations that Management:
a. 	Stop the practice of giving different rates to JOs having the same position in the LGU in order not to create inequality in the workforce of the LGU; and
b. 	Require the HRMO to adopt a schedule of standard rates for JOs per position that is based on qualification and the nature of work/assignment taking into consideration the prevailing minimum wage in the locality.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Management strictly observe the proper charging of salaries and wages of JO personnel, to the specific office or LEEs where they are assigned to attain the proper matching of income and expenses and the fair presentation of the financial statements. Further, consider the options provided under LBC No. 100 if there are LEEs determined to be not economically viable.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality stop the hiring of street sweepers as they perform the same services already being rendered by the barangay street sweepers.
We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the rules and regulations in procuring consulting services under the RIRR of RA 9184 whenever hiring a private lawyer to act as a consultant.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Submit all lacking documents relating to the cash advance and liquidation documents for the salaries and wages of JO and COS personnel as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445; and
b. 	Make sure that the JO/COS personnel with a valid Job Order contract should be the ones to render his/her duties and responsibilities, and sign in the payroll and Accomplishment Report.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management ensure that the certification as to availability of funds must first be secured before hiring JOs to protect both the interests of the LGU and the JO personnel.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Conduct a thorough evaluation of documents submitted by applicants prior to hiring;
b. 	Strictly adhere to Sections 123 and 124 of PD No. 1445 on the implementation of a sound internal control system particularly on the recruitment process of the Municipality in order to avoid situations of hiring personnel who are already employed or hired by other agencies; and
c. 	Require the concerned personnel to refund the amount of P59,699.94 paid to him as salaries/wages for the period July 1 to September 30, 2022 as it constitutes double compensation.

We recommended that Management come up with a workable solution in complying with the mandated daily minimum wage rate prescribed by the RTWPB for Region 5 given the limited resources of the Municipality such as reviewing the propriety of the number of JOs being hired.

We recommended the Management to adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts to the community.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE direct the LSB to work on improving the formulation and utilization of the SEF Budget geared towards its responsiveness to the needs of the local schools under its jurisdiction, as its responsibility delineated under DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017. Further, that the LSB formulate and approve the SEF Budget for the ensuing year not later than October 31 of the current year as provided in the said JC.

The Audit Team recommended that the BAC:
a. 	Comply strictly with the provisions of the relevant RIRR of RA No. 9184 on evaluating bids and prepare the Abstract of Bids in accordance with Section 32.2.4 of RA No. 9184; and
b. 	Perform diligently the procedures required by the relevant RIRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the bidding process employed will result in the award of contracts to the most qualified and complying bidder.
We recommended that the Municipality issue a Supplemental Bid/Bulletin in order to clarify or modify any provision of the bidding documents in accordance with Section 22.5 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.
We recommended the Municipality to require all winning bidders to post the required performance security in accordance with Section 39 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipality: (i) submit plausible explanations for all the deficiencies noted and lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit; (ii) strictly comply with the provisions of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 on the procurement of various infrastructure projects with regard to post-qualification, contract award and contract signing, among others, to enhance transparency and establish regularity of the procurement process and award of contracts.

We recommended that the Municipality comply strictly with the provision of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR particularly on the completion of procurement process within the required logical occurrence of activities.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality submit all lacking documents relating to the procurement of various infrastructure projects as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

The Audit Team recommended that the Municipality address this issue on ownership of the location of the project to protect the interest of the LGU. Henceforth, for the Municipality to secure a notarized deed of sale or deed of donation on the property where the project will be constructed prior to the award of the contract in accordance with Section 17.6 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management continue to improve practices in programming, prioritizing and implementing programs and projects to maximize the utilization of the Fund in attaining desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the community.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Planning and Development Council to adhere to the guidelines set under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, regarding the classification of projects.

We recommended that the Municipality through the Sangguniang Bayan enact  a supplemental budget whenever modifying specific program, projects and activities (PPAs) covered by appropriations prior to implementing new PPAs pursuant to Section 447(2)(i) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Direct the MPDC to closely coordinate with the Engineering Office as to the status or percentage of completion of the project as well as the other necessary information to be presented in the accomplishment report;
b. 	Conduct thorough check and review to ensure accurate, consistent, and reliable reporting of PPAs; and
c. 	Provide the Audit Team at the beginning of the year with a list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities ("PPA") and those that are to be implemented during the year with all the necessary information required per Section 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management may either:
a. 	Undertake public bidding for the procurement of POL and treat the payment as an inventory of POL stored within the business premises of the supplier and not as cash deposit, thereby agreeing upon the quantity of stored POL (number of liters in the case of diesel and gasoline) subject to withdrawal by the LGU and not affected by any fluctuations in oil prices; or
b. 	Arrange an on-credit procurement of POL under a periodic billing system with a reputable supplier to do away with advance payment, as allowed per Section 53.14 of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	Ensure proper coordination between the HRMO and Accounting Office with regard to processing of loan applications with lending institutions and to adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and other payroll deductions such that the monthly net take home pay of any Municipal employee does not fall below the prescribed ceiling of ₱5,000.00; and
b. 	Limit the payroll deductions to the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee's net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay of ₱5,000.00. In the event that loan payments can no longer be accommodated due to take home pay becoming less than P5,000.00,  the employee-borrower shall directly make payment arrangement with the lending institution to avoid penalties/surcharges.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of financial reports and accounts by:
a. 	Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
b. 	Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
c. 	Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.
We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of the Trial Balances and the Financial Statements.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We recommended that the Management establish the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth under DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of office to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:

The Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant with the List of Delinquent Market Stallholders with amount due and penalties before the end of each year to facilitate timely recording of appropriate receivable and income accounts; and

The Municipal Accountant to recognize the total unpaid rentals, business taxes, surcharges and interests as of December 31, 2018 in the books of the Municipality to the appropriate receivable and income/equity account, in compliance with the provisions of PPSAS 13 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Municipality.
observations.reason: Cannot recognize receivable because no amount of receivable was provided by the MTO. Also, recognition of income that are not 100% collectible is not prudent.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to establish the useful life of the Infrastructure Assets accounts and compute, recognize and record in the books of accounts the corresponding depreciation expenses.

Require the Accountable Officers and concerned offices to immediately submit the complete documentary requirements to enable the evaluation of their requests for relief from accountability; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose the lost and damaged properties in the Notes to Financial Statements.

Cause the preparation of the IIRUP by the Property Officer/Unit to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties; and
Direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable.
We recommended that Management reclassify the CIP-Building amounting to ₱59,363,047.35 to the Building account and compute and record its related depreciation from the period of completion.
We recommended that Management submit immediately the lacking required supporting documents to sufficiently establish the validity of the transaction.

We recommended that Management submit the Journal Entry Voucher recording the adjustments for the variance of ₱2,501,338.52 between the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds as at December 31, 2021, for review by the Audit Team.
We recommended that henceforth the Office of the Municipal Accountant ensure that balances of reciprocal accounts are reconciled at year-end and that thorough and careful review and analysis of transactions be made to avoid errors in account balances.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to orient the Disbursing Officers on the maintenance of cashbook and accompanying records pursuant to Section 28 of the Manual on NGAS for Local Government Units, Volume II and Section 6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended Management to re-evaluate the placement of time deposit as investment or terminate and utilize these funds for projects that could immediately benefit the constituents. Otherwise, the Local Chief Executive has to secure an authority from the SB to support the investment of the time deposit and provide a financial analysis to establish that these funds are indeed idle.

We recommended that Management:
Limit the appropriation and disbursement of funds for Representation Expenses to the limitation prescribed under Section 325(h) of RA 7160 which is two percent of the actual real property tax collection in the next preceding year; and
Refrain from appropriating budget for Representation Expenses to other municipal offices since only the LCE is allowed pursuant to Section 325(h) of RA 7160.

We recommended that Management submit certified balance of leave credits of the retirees as well as certified true copies of the above-mentioned documentary requirements in support of the payment for terminal leave benefits.
We recommended that henceforth Management support all transactions with valid, complete and proper documents as required under Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct Ms. Morada to refund the honoraria received as BOT member and that she be relieved as BOT member in compliance with the requirement under CHED Memorandum Order No. 42 series of 2016. We further recommend that the LGU be circumspect in appointing individuals to positions in committees/boards to avoid payment of irregular expenditures.

In line with the foregoing observation, we recommended that Management:
Submit through electronic mail a copy of its APP and PMR to the GPPB, in both Microsoft Excel and PDF File; and
Post the APP and PMR in the agency website or in a conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the Municipality and submit a certification indicating that it has complied with either of the said posting requirements to the GPPB.

We recommended that the agency prepare its APCPI and immediately submit a copy to GPPB TSO. Whereas, APCPI results are among the required documents to be submitted to GPPB for the grant of Performance-Based Bonus every year starting CY 2015.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to keep abreast of updates in Philippine Bidding Documents (PBDs) and Required Forms and thus specifically ensure that Philippine Bidding Documents made available to prospective bidders are in accordance with the 6th edition of the PBDs.
We recommended that the Agency enter into contract within 10 calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the NOA as required under Section 37.2.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Agency conduct a survey to confirm the exclusivity of the source and make sure that all other conditions required under the RIRR of RA No. 9184 was met before undergoing alternative mode of procurement.
We recommended that the Agency post the NOA, contract/PO and NTP in accordance with the provisions of Section 54.3 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184; and refrain from entering into contract/PO without prior issuance of NOA and NTP.
We recommended that the Agency require suppliers to provide warranty securities in an amount and form prescribed per Section 62.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184 for alternative method of procurement thru Direct Contracting.

We recommended that the LCE, in consultation with the concerned department heads of the BAC, Accounting, Budget and Engineering, be able to provide the Audit Team with the basis and necessary reference documents attached thereto such as planning documents for the lot classification of component infrastructure projects procured in CY 2021.
Otherwise, we recommended that the Municipality with the LCE as directly responsible to install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control to craft a policy for the same premised on the PD 1445 to address the risk on the timeliness and efficiency of project implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineering Office conduct a satisfactory site inspection of all infrastructure projects in order to come up with appropriate design plans and program of works to minimize if not avoid variation orders.
Further, we recommended that the LCE direct the department head and concerned officials in the Municipal Engineering Office to make the needed review of its procedures in the project planning to address deficiencies noted as per audit.

We recommended that the Municipality under the direction of the LCE:
Ensure that Certificate of Final Acceptance for the infrastructure projects be issued only upon the lapse of one-year period from date of its completion;
Direct the Municipal Engineering Office through its Department Head and inspectorate team or counterpart thereto to conduct necessary and thorough determination of any resulting defects occurring within the periods mentioned, defects liability period to the structural defects period for the filing of appropriate claims with the contractor and parties liable in accordance with Sections 62.2.2 and 62.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the R.A. No. 9184; and
Provide the Audit Team with the result of the preceding recommended action on the determination of any resulting defects during the defects liability period until the structural defects period.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to exercise care and caution in accepting and verifying bonds by bidders and contractors required in view of the procurement activity.
Also, we recommended that the BAC present documents or copies thereof it may have used to ascertain that the insurance company appearing in the Negative List as published in the GPPB website is not the issuer of the bonds for the subject procurement activities.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to perform its function in strict compliance with what is mandated per RA 9184 and its IRR to impress upon stakeholders that procurement activities conducted are aboveboard.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF to cover all the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management in order to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts to the community. Further, for Management to include in the Annual Investment Plan/LDRRM Plan the programs/projects/activities to be charged against the Special Trust Fund-LDRRMF.

We recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose separately in the Notes to the Financial Statements the amount and details of the unexpended/unobligated balance of LDRRMF.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper charging of expenditures to the LDRRMF to ensure that the funds are utilized in accordance with its intended purposes.

We recommended that Management carefully review unexpended balances of appropriations at the end of the year and determine its propriety as a continuing appropriation or to be reverted and made available for other priority projects of the LGU allowed as chargeable expenses under the 20 per cent DF.

We recommended that the LCE direct the LSB to work on improving the formulation and utilization of the SEF Budget geared towards its responsiveness to the needs of the local schools under its jurisdiction, as its responsibility delineated under DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that the Agency identify specific individuals to represent the above-mentioned four sectors in full compliance with the required organizational structure of the SWMB under the RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Agency consider preparing a comprehensive work plan for SWM operation indicating all activities needed to efficiently and effectively discharge the function with corresponding budget per activity. This would help the Municipality in determining the sources of funds and the amount to be budgeted to attain the objective of SWM.

We recommended that the MENRO prepare the required reports on the performance status and analysis of implementation and enforcement of solid waste management ordinance pursuant to Section 2 of Rule II of Executive Order No. 46, Series of 2021.
We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of the Monthly Trial Balances.

We recommended that Management further improve its performance in the submission of financial reports and accounts by:
Requiring the concerned Accountable Officer to submit the accounts and financial reports to the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to the timelines of the abovementioned existing laws, rules and regulations;
Directing the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office all the disbursements vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, including the corresponding reports for each month on or before the fifth day of the ensuing month or pursuant to the abovementioned pertinent provisions of rules and regulations, as applicable; and
Directing the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts to the Auditor within ten days of the ensuing month.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit within the prescribed period the BRS and the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), paid checks and original copies of Debit and Credit Memos, for review and verification.

We recommended that Management strictly observe    the guidelines in the utilization of cash donations in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We also recommended that the control procedures be adopted among concerned offices/units for easy coordination and proper monitoring of donations.

We recommended that the Municipality adhere to the mandatory posting on the GPPB Online Portal of information relative to Emergency Procurements under the Bayanihan Act, to ensure that accountability and transparency in the procurement is upheld.

We recommended that the Management:

Compel the MSWD Officer to fast track the submission of the Relief Distribution Lists indicating the names of recipients and their signatures or, at the very least, signed by the Punong Barangay as recipient in behalf of the constituents, otherwise, non-submission thereof would warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and 

Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation to protect the interests of the government and to prevent unwarranted disbursements of public funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary reversing entry recording the Accounts Payable.  Henceforth, strictly observe the proper recognition of Accounts Payable by crediting this account only when the goods are delivered or services are rendered pursuant to NGAS for LGUs, Vol. III.

We recommended that Management:

 Ensure that the date of receipt of PO by the supplier be indicated to determine required date of delivery;  

Impose liquidated damages for delayed deliveries; and

Avail of the remedies under Section III.1.a of Annex H of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 for contracts with undelivered goods amounting to at least ten per cent of the contract price.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the validity of the unliquidated obligations or Accounts Payable amounting to ₱3.61 million which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no legitimate claim against the Municipality, cause the reversion of the same to the government equity.

Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to record the reconciling items in the books of accounts based on the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) prepared. We recommended that the Management avail of the remedy under Section 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the withholding of salaries of employees with unliquidated cash advances in order to compel them to settle their cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper charging of expenditures to the 30 percent Quick Response Fund and 70 percent Mitigation Fund to ensure that the funds are utilized in accordance with its intended purposes.

Approve requests for extension of contract time by contractors strictly in accordance with the conditions prescribed under Section 11 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184;

Ensure that requests for extension of contract time be supported with the written consent of the bondsmen and that the validity of the Performance Security be correspondingly extended; and

Impose liquidated damages for works not completed within the approved contract time, including time extensions that are allowable under Section 10 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipality continue implementing GAD programs, projects and activities to attain its objective of gender equality and contribute to the empowerment of its women constituents. Henceforth, require the MPDO to prepare the LDRRMFIP incorporating PPAs chargeable against the prior year’s unexpended balance of LDRRMF and other sources to optimally utilize all available funds.

The BAC to strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and ensure that the contract or PO is awarded to a duly authorized distributor/supplier at prices most advantageous to the government; and
Procuring Entity to properly plan its procurement activities to avoid duplication of purchases of similar goods or splitting of contract/PO; and, the LDRRMC take cognizant of the existing declaration of the State of Calamity of the LGU due to dengue outbreak and make the necessary recommendation for its termination.
